revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:阿賀神社]]
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name=Aga Shrine | location={{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|qid=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}}, {{ill|Higashiōmi|en|Higashiōmi|qid=Q240891|lt=Higashiōmi|1=Higashiōmi}}, Kowaki Town 2247 | deity={{ill|Oshihomimi|qid=Q462555|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}} | shrine_Rank=Former {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=近代社格制度|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}・{{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|qid=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine|1=Beppyo shrine}} | founded=Era of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}} | main_hall=Gongen-zukuri with forward-facing porch | annual_festival={{ill|March 24|en|March 24|qid=Q2453|lt=March 24|1=March 24}} | image=File:240608 Aga-jinja Higashiomi Shiga pref Japan06s3.jpg | latitude_deg=35|Rank_min=07|Rank_sec=04 | longitude_deg=136|longitude_min=10|longitude_sec=54 | map=Japan Shiga | prefecture={{ill|Higashiomi|qid=Q240891|1=Higashiōmi}} | coordinates={{Coord|35.117777777777775|N|136.18166666666667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Aga Shrine''' (阿賀神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|qid=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}}, {{ill|Higashiōmi|en|Higashiōmi|qid=Q240891|lt=Higashiōmi|1=Higashiōmi}}, Kowaki Town. It is commonly known as '''Tarōbō-gū''' (太郎坊宮) or '''Tarōbō Aga Shrine''' (太郎坊阿賀神社). The former shrine Rank was {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=近代社格制度|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}, and it is currently a {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|qid=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine|1=Beppyo shrine}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|qid=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. == History == === Shinbutsu Shūgō === [[File:Taroubougu 1.JPG|150px|thumb|Meoto Iwa (Husband and Wife Rocks)]] Originally, large rocks including the {{ill|Meoto Iwa|en|Meoto Iwa|qid=Q333875|lt=Meoto Iwa|1=Meoto Iwa}} were revered as {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|qid=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura (Shinto)|1=Iwakura rock}}, and rituals were conducted there during the era of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}}. In the era of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}}, around {{ill|600 AD|ja|西暦600年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/600|comment=likely broken Sexagenary year|lt=600 AD|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135500699|1=600}}, {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} established {{ill|Kawarayadera|ja|瓦屋寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawarayadera|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawarayadera|1=Kawarayazen-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q85882405}} on Mount Mizukura to bake tiles for use at {{ill|Shitennō-ji|en|Shitennō-ji|qid=Q339859|lt=Shitennō-ji|1=Shitennō-ji}} in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|qid=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}}, and around the same time, he built a shrine on Mount Aka (a peak of Mount Mizukura) to enshrine {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|qid=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}. The shrine was named Aga Shrine after the name of Mount Aka. The local place name, Kowaki, is said to be derived from the story that {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} always held her grandson, Ame-no-Oshihomimi, close to her side, calling him "This side child," and he descended to this place. In the 18th year of {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|qid=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}} ({{ill|799|en|799|qid=Q33488|lt=799|1=799}}), {{ill|Saichō|en|Saichō|qid=Q449943|lt=Saichō|1=Saichō}}, impressed by the divine virtue of Aga Shrine, established {{ill|Jōgan-ji (Higashiōmi)|ja|成願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan-ji (Higashiōmi)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan-ji (Higashiōmi)|1=Jōgan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11495805}} at the foot of the shrine, with {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} as its principal deity, making it the {{ill|Jingū-ji|en|Jingū-ji|qid=Q1074971|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji}} of Aga Shrine. At that time, the tengu {{ill|Tarōbō|ja|太郎坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarōbō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tarōbō|1=Tarōbō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11444521}}, a senior disciple of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=役小角|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=役小角|1=En no Gyōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q198946}} who lived on Mount Aka, appeared to Saichō and helped him establish a temple on the mountain as its guardian deity. Aga Shrine and Jōgan-ji, a temple of the {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|qid=Q668188|lt=Tendai sect|1=Tiantai}}, eventually merged, and over 50 shrines and monks' quarters were built, including Takenakabō, Uebō, Matsumotobō, Gyōmanbō, Ishigakibō, and Ōizumibō. The town of {{ill|Yōkaichi|ja|八日市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōkaichi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōkaichi|1=Yōkaichi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11391245}}, which developed around the market held on the 8th, the enshrined day of Yakushi Nyorai, flourished greatly. Jōgan-ji created the Okunoin at the highest point within Aga Shrine's grounds, enshrining a statue of Tarōbō Daigongen. As a result, Aga Shrine came under Jōgan-ji's management, and the combined name of Tarōbō-gū became commonly used, turning it into a sacred site for {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}}. {{ill|Sasaki Nariyori|ja|佐々木成頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Nariyori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Nariyori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135501971|1=Sasaki Nariyori}} of the {{ill|Ōmi Genji|ja|近江源氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Genji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Genji|1=Omi Genji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11638504}} clan built the Kowaki residence at the foot of Mount Aka, and later constructed Kowaki Castle on Mount Kowaki beside Mount Aka. Due to the prosperity of Yōkaichi, the area became a central region of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|qid=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}. The head of the {{ill|Sasaki clan|en|Sasaki clan|qid=Q1028587|lt=Sasaki clan|1=Sasaki clan}}, the {{ill|Rokkaku clan|en|Rokkaku clan|qid=Q2975342|lt=Rokkaku clan|1=Rokkaku clan}}'s head {{ill|Rokkaku Yoshikata|ja|六角頼綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Yoshikata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Yoshikata|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=佐々木頼綱|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=佐々木頼綱|1=Sasaki Yoritsuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11382448}}, moved his base to Kanedaya mansion (Kongōji Castle), and later, during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|qid=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, {{ill|Rokkaku Takayori|ja|六角高頼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkaku Takayori|1=Rokkaku Takayori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2890348}} moved to {{ill|Kannonji Castle|en|Kannonji Castle|qid=Q2969462|lt=Kannonji Castle|1=Kannonji Castle}} on Mount Kannon. However, the importance of the area remained unchanged until it was engulfed in the flames of the conflict between {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}, who advanced to Kyoto with {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiaki|en|Ashikaga Yoshiaki|qid=Q434415|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiaki|1=Ashikaga Yoshiaki}}, and {{ill|Rokkaku Yoshikata|en|Rokkaku Yoshikata|qid=Q282529|lt=Rokkaku Yoshikata|1=Rokkaku Yoshikata}} in the 11th year of {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|qid=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku|1=Eiroku}} ({{ill|1568|en|1568|qid=Q6648|lt=1568|1=1568}}). After that, the ruined Tarōbō-gū was occupied by {{ill|Sugitani Zenjūbō|en|Sugitani Zenjūbō|qid=Q11095190|lt=Sugizumi-bō|1=Sugitani Zenjūbō}}, who later attempted to assassinate Nobunaga at {{ill|Chigusa-kaidō|ja|千種街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigusa-kaidō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigusa-kaidō|1=Chikusa-kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q22130131}}, and his group. [[File:Aga-Shrine（Tarobogu）-Meoto-Rock01.jpg|thumb|Meoto Iwa at Aga Shrine (Tarōbō-gū)]] === Shinbutsu Bunri === Aga Shrine was soon rebuilt, but Jōgan-ji was not restored until the 17th year of {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|qid=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei|1=Kan'ei}} ({{ill|1640|en|1640|qid=Q6901|lt=1640|1=1640}}), when Hōjuin Gyōshō and his disciple Yūsei built a main hall and bell tower. However, the revival of the monks' quarters was not as extensive as before. In the {{ill|Enpō|en|Enpō|qid=Q1145735|lt=Empō|1=Enpō}} era ({{ill|1673|en|1673|qid=Q7617|lt=1673|1=1673}} - {{ill|1681|en|1681|qid=Q7645|lt=1681|1=1681}}), Jōgan-ji and the villagers disputed over the use of the communal land around Mount Aka, and Jōgan-ji sued the villagers to the shogunate. For over 70 years, the two sides continued to quarrel until the villagers finally won. As a result, the shrine priests of Aga Shrine were appointed anew by the villagers and {{ill|Ujiko|ja|氏子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujiko|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=氏神|comment=changed title based on wikidata|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=氏神|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=ujigami}} on an annual rotating basis, thus separating Aga Shrine from the control of Jōgan-ji. In the 3rd year of {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|qid=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki|1=Hōreki}} ({{ill|1753|en|1753|qid=Q7621|lt=1753|1=1753}}), Jōgan-ji moved several Buddhist treasures, including the Tarōbō Daigongen statue from the Okunoin to the main hall at the foot of the mountain, calling it Tarōbō Daigongen. Aga Shrine then converted the Okunoin of Jōgan-ji into its new main hall and adopted the name Tarōbō-gū. Although still in the form of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|qid=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, Aga Shrine and Jōgan-ji thus separated. In {{ill|1868|en|1868|qid=Q7717|lt=1868|1=1868}} (first year of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}), the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} edict completely separated Aga Shrine and Jōgan-ji. In {{ill|1872|en|1872|qid=Q7753|lt=Meiji 5|1=1872}}, Shugendō was abolished, and in {{ill|1876|en|1876|qid=Q7766|lt=Meiji 9|1=1876}}, the name Tarōbō-gū was regulated, with Aga Shrine becoming the official name. However, the name Tarōbō-gū continued to be used colloquially. The shrine was Ranked as a {{ill|Village shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=近代社格制度|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}. In {{ill|1909|en|1909|qid=Q2057|lt=Meiji 42|1=1909}}, the deities of ten nearby shrines were enshrined in the same hall, and in {{ill|1924|en|1924|qid=Q2173|lt=Taisho 12|1=1924}}, the main hall was rebuilt. After World War II, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. In {{ill|1962|en|1962|qid=Q2764|lt=Showa 37|1=1962}}, the Tarōbō Industrial Road was built by the {{ill|Japan Ground Self-Defense Force|en|Japan Ground Self-Defense Force|qid=Q889751|lt=Japan Ground Self-Defense Force|1=Japan Ground Self-Defense Force}}, allowing cars to directly reach Aga Shrine via Jōgan-ji. The road was later extended to {{ill|Kawarayadera|ja|瓦屋寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawarayadera|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawarayadera|1=Kawarayazen-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q85882405}}. In {{ill|2007|en|2007|qid=Q2024|lt=Heisei 19|1=2007}}, Aga Shrine was selected as one of the Eight Views of Higashiōmi by the city. In {{ill|2013|en|2013|qid=Q1998|lt=Heisei 25|1=2013}}, the Meoto Iwa was designated a natural monument of Higashiōmi. == Relationship with nearby Aga Shrines == There are other shrines with the same name near the well-known Aga Shrine at Tarōbō-gū. There is a theory that these shrines were built to worship the deity of Mount Aka through the large rocks in the precincts. The view of Tarōbō-gū is that worshiping Mount Aka as sacred and conducting rituals nearby eventually led to the development of these shrines.<ref>Reference to "Tracing the History of Tarōbō-gū Faith" by the Tarōbō-gū Editorial Committee, pages 45-46. This book is available for purchase at the shrine office without an ISBN.</ref> == Deities == * Main deity - {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|qid=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}} * Enshrined deities - {{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=えびす|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=えびす}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|天満大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=菅原道真|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=菅原道真|1=Sugawara no Michizane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q382005}}, {{ill|Daishōgun (directional deity)|ja|大将軍大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daishōgun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大神|1=Sanno Gongen|2=ja|3=日吉大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshi Taisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshi Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135521683}}, Jūzenji Ōkami, Shimizu Ōkami, Gosha Ōkami (Tennōmiya, Jūzenji Gongenmiya, Shinmeimiya, Iwagamimiya, Hachimanmiya) == Grounds == * Main hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in the 3rd year of {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|qid=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki|1=Hōreki}} ({{ill|1753|en|1753|qid=Q7621|lt=1753|1=1753}}). Gongen-zukuri style with a copper roof and forward-facing porch. Originally the Okunoin of Jōgan-ji. Connected to the worship hall. A stage with a cantilevered structure is in front. * Worship hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in {{ill|1924|en|1924|qid=Q2173|lt=Taisho 12|1=1924}}. * Stage (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in {{ill|1880|en|1880|qid=Q7806|lt=Meiji 13|1=1880}}. Renovated in {{ill|1948|en|1948|qid=Q5165|lt=Showa 23|1=1948}}, with only the floor made of concrete. * Offering hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in the mid-Meiji period. * Meoto Iwa (Natural Monument of Higashiōmi) - Located in front of the main hall. The male rock is on the mountain side, and the female rock is on the valley side. The path is 12 meters long and 80 cm wide. It is said that those with ill intentions will be pinched between the rocks while passing through. It is also said that a tengu resides here. * Koshikake Iwa - It is said that {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|en|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|qid=Q310445|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune|1=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}} sat on this rock when he visited on his way from {{ill|Kurama-dera|en|Kurama-dera|qid=Q608668|lt=Kurama-dera|1=Kurama-dera}} to {{ill|Historic Monuments and Sites of Hiraizumi|en|Historic Monuments and Sites of Hiraizumi|qid=Q2896261|lt=Hiraizumi|1=Historic Monuments and Sites of Hiraizumi}} to meet {{ill|Fujiwara no Hidehira|en|Fujiwara no Hidehira|qid=Q3090625|lt=Fujiwara no Hidehira|1=Fujiwara no Hidehira}}. He prayed for the revival and victory of the Genji clan. * Hibuse Inari - Futami Shrine and Jūnisha Shrine. * Akagami Inari Shrine * Akagami Atago Shrine * Worship hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in the late Edo period. * Kaguraden (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in the mid-Meiji period. * Eian-den (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in {{ill|1907|en|1907|qid=Q2048|lt=Meiji 40|1=1907}}. Former shrine office. * Ryūjin Shrine (Chōzuya, Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - deity: {{ill|Ryūjin|ja|龍神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūjin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūjin|qq=}}. Rebuilt in {{ill|1911|en|1911|qid=Q2076|lt=Meiji 44|1=1911}}. * Chōraku-den (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in {{ill|1930|en|1930|qid=Q18787|lt=Showa 5|1=1930}}. Lecture hall. * Saikiko (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in {{ill|1938|en|1938|qid=Q18645|lt=Showa 13|1=1938}}. * Sanshū-den - Four-story shrine office, rest area, and training dojo built in {{ill|1973|en|1973|qid=Q2477|lt=Showa 48|1=1973}}. * {{ill|Jishu Shrine|ja|地主神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jishu Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jishu Shrine|1=Jishu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10928238}} * {{ill|Seven Lucky Gods|en|Seven Lucky Gods|qid=Q846858|lt=Seven Lucky Gods|1=Seven Lucky Gods}} statues * Ichigan Jōjūsha - Wish-fulfilling hall. * Yume Shrine * Tengu statue * {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=役小角|ja_comment=former now fixed redirect|3=役小角|1=En no Gyōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q198946}} statue * Shibi Shrine * Akagami Fudō Myōō * Fudō Myōō worship place (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in {{ill|1930|en|1930|qid=Q18787|lt=Showa 5|1=1930}}. * Ema-den (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in {{ill|1925|en|1925|qid=Q18107|lt=Taisho 13|1=1925}}. * Prayer hall - Enshrines a divided spirit of the main deity. * Chūmon (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in {{ill|1934|en|1934|qid=Q18714|lt=Showa 9|1=1934}}. == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main hall * Worship hall * Stage * Offering hall * Worship hall * Kaguraden * Eian-den * Chōraku-den * Saikiko * Chōzuya * Copper Torii - Built in {{ill|1957|en|1957|qid=Q5311|lt=Showa 32|1=1957}}. * Ema-den * Fudō Myōō worship place * Chūmon * First Torii - Rebuilt in {{ill|1894|en|1894|qid=Q7843|lt=Meiji 27|1=1894}}. === Natural Monuments of Higashiōmi === * Meoto Iwa of Aga Shrine (Tarōbō-gū) == festivals == * Ohitaki Taisai: Held annually on the first Sunday of December<ref>[http://www.higashiomi.net/youkaichi/450.html Ohitaki Taisai]</ref>. == Notes == {{Reflist|qq=}} == References == * Tarōbō-gū Editorial Committee, "Tracing the History of Tarōbō-gū Faith", Tarōbō・Aga Shrine Office, 2017. == See also == * {{ill|Mount Aka|ja|赤神山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Aka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Aka|1=Mount Akagami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q24865038}} * {{ill|Jōgan-ji (Higashiōmi)|ja|成願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan-ji (Higashiōmi)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgan-ji (Higashiōmi)|1=Jōgan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11495805}} * {{ill|Tarōbōgū-mae Station|en|Tarōbōgū-mae Station|qid=Q7687071|lt=Tarōbō-gūmae Station|1=Tarōbōgū-mae Station}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Aga-jinja, Higashiomi|阿賀神社|qq=}} *[http://www.tarobo.sakura.ne.jp/ Official site of Tarōbō-gū] (official site) *[http://www.tarobo.sakura.ne.jp/img/tarobo2.pdf Pamphlet of Tarōbō・Aga Shrine] Accessed on August 8, 2019 {{Shinto-stub|qq=}} {{Beppyo Shrines|qq=}}
{{citation needed|ja|阿賀神社|version=102029703|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:阿賀神社]]
